preamble.summary: Enoch prophesies that in the days of sinners, the years will be shortened, rain will be withheld, the moon will alter its order, and the stars will transgress their courses. The natural order will be disrupted because of human sin. People will mistake stars for gods and evil will multiply.
preamble.remarkable: This is the Astronomical Book's theological climax. The 364-day calendar is not merely a timekeeping system — it is a covenant. When sinners corrupt the calendar (presumably by adopting a different one), the cosmos itself responds with disorder. This is the inverse of Genesis 8:22 ('while the earth remains, seedtime and harvest shall not cease'): covenant unfaithfulness breaks the promise. The chapter directly polemicizes against the 365-day Egyptian calendar and the 354-day lunar calendar.
preamble.friction: The claim that cosmic disorder follows from calendrical error inverts the normal causation: most ancient peoples assumed that observing the heavens determined the calendar, not that the calendar determined the heavens. The Enochic view is radically theological — human obedience to the correct calendar sustains cosmic order.
preamble.connections: Isaiah 24:4-6 — the earth polluted by those who transgress the everlasting covenant. Hosea 4:3 — the land mourns because of human sin. Romans 8:20-22 — creation subjected to futility. Revelation 8:12 — a third of the sun, moon, and stars struck. Jubilees 6:36-37 — the prophecy that Israel will forget the calendar.

verses.text_reference: And in those days the angel Uriel answered and said to me: 'Behold, I have shown thee everything, Enoch, and I have revealed everything to thee that thou shouldst see this sun and this moon, and the leaders of the stars of the heaven and all those who turn them, their tasks and times and departures.'
verses.rendering: In those days the angel Uriel answered and said to me: 'Look — I have shown you everything, Enoch. I have revealed everything to you so that you should see this sun and this moon, the leaders of the stars of heaven and all who turn them, their tasks, their times, and their departures.'
translator_notes: Uriel's declaration that 'everything' has been shown marks the transition from revelation to prophecy. The astronomical instruction is complete; now comes the warning about what happens when it is ignored.

verses.text_reference: 'And in the days of the sinners the years shall be shortened, and their seed shall be tardy on their lands and fields, and all things on the earth shall alter, and shall not appear in their time: and the rain shall be kept back and the heaven shall withhold it.'
verses.rendering: 'In the days of the sinners the years will be shortened. Their crops will be late on their lands and fields, and all things on earth will change and will not appear at their proper time. The rain will be held back, and heaven will withhold it.'
translator_notes: Shortened years — whether meaning fewer productive days or a disrupted calendar — lead to agricultural failure. The connection between correct timekeeping and successful farming was not abstract: planting and harvesting at the wrong times meant starvation. The calendar is a matter of survival.

verses.text_reference: 'And in those times the fruits of the earth shall be backward, and shall not grow in their time, and the fruits of the trees shall be withheld in their time.'
verses.rendering: 'In those times the fruits of the earth will be delayed and will not grow in their season, and the fruits of the trees will be withheld at their proper time.'
translator_notes: The delayed harvest is both literal (wrong calendar leads to mistimed agriculture) and theological (creation withholds its bounty when the covenant is broken). Deuteronomy 28:23-24 describes the same punishment for covenant violation.

verses.text_reference: 'And the moon shall alter her order, and not appear at her time.'
verses.rendering: 'The moon will alter its order and not appear at its proper time.'
translator_notes: Lunar disruption — the moon failing to keep its appointed schedule — is the cosmic sign of calendrical sin. If the moon 'changes its order,' the lunar-based festivals celebrated by the Jerusalem temple would be thrown into confusion.

verses.text_reference: 'And in those days the sun shall be seen and he shall journey in the evening on the extremity of the great chariot in the west and shall shine more brightly than accords with the order of light.'
verses.rendering: 'In those days the sun will be seen traveling in the evening at the edge of the great chariot in the west, and it will shine more brightly than the order of light allows.'
translator_notes: An unnaturally bright sunset — the sun exceeding its appointed brightness — is a sign of cosmic transgression. The luminaries themselves will violate their own laws, mirroring the humans who have abandoned the divine calendar.

verses.text_reference: 'And many chiefs of the stars shall transgress the order (prescribed). And these shall alter their orbits and tasks, and not appear at the seasons prescribed to them.'
verses.rendering: 'Many leaders of the stars will transgress the prescribed order. They will alter their orbits and tasks and will not appear at the times appointed for them.'
translator_notes: Stellar transgression parallels angelic rebellion — the same vocabulary used for the Watchers' sin in chapters 6-16 is applied to stars that leave their courses. In Enochic cosmology, stars and angels are closely identified (cf. 1 Enoch 18:14-16, where fallen stars are imprisoned).

verses.text_reference: 'And the whole order of the stars shall be concealed from the sinners, and the thoughts of those on the earth shall err concerning them, and they shall be altered from all their ways, yea, they shall err and take them to be gods.'
verses.rendering: 'The whole order of the stars will be hidden from sinners. The thoughts of those on earth will go astray concerning them — they will be led into error in all their ways and will even take the stars to be gods.'
translator_notes: The ultimate calendrical sin: astral worship. When people lose the correct calendar, they lose the understanding that stars are servants, not sovereigns. They begin worshipping the creation rather than the Creator — the same critique Paul makes in Romans 1:25.

key_terms.note: The Astronomical Book's sharpest polemic: incorrect calendar knowledge leads ultimately to idolatry — worshipping stars instead of the God who commands them

verses.text_reference: 'And evil shall be multiplied upon them, and punishment shall come upon them so as to destroy all.'
verses.rendering: 'Evil will multiply against them, and punishment will come upon them to destroy them all.'
translator_notes: The chapter ends with total judgment. Calendrical sin is not a minor infraction but a covenant-breaking offense that merits destruction. The severity echoes the flood judgment — the first time God destroyed a world that had fallen into disorder.
